diary: Woke up to that grey, drizzly Tuesday feeling, the kind where the whole world looks like it’s been lightly washed. From Sammamish I checked ferry and commute options first thing, then watched SR-520 like it was a temperamental machine—eastbound closed 9 to noon, and I could already feel the traffic pressure creeping into my thoughts. By the time I rolled into Bothell, the rain had turned steady, so I mostly lived indoors: turbine inspections, paperwork, and that familiar rhythm of staying dry while keeping everything in my head lined up. I ducked into MOD Pizza for a warm lunch at Downtown Bothell, then wrapped up final checks and gear-list planning. After a quick Safeway restock, I took a safer cross-lake route home. Evening was quiet—friends at Fremont Brewing Company, then vinyl hunting at Norman’s Rare Guitars in Ballard. I’m tired, but content, like the day clicked into place.
thought_process: I chose the commute options based on the SR-520 closure window, not just to save time but to keep my focus steady—when roads get chaotic, my work brain tends to drag behind. The rain from 7am to 2pm basically dictated the day: more indoor paperwork between site tasks, quicker errands, and fewer “let’s just see” detours. I grabbed a warm lunch to avoid that afternoon slump where coordination falls apart, and I kept the grocery run minimal so I wouldn’t waste energy. The world events—bridge repair and traffic snarls—definitely made me more deliberate, less spontaneous. My mood shifted from cautious in the morning to calmer once the inspections were done, then brighter with friends and vinyl. By night, I felt settled, like I’d handled the day instead of letting it handle me.
world_event_prompt: Grey, drizzly Tuesday with steady rain from 7am to 2pm, high of 58F. SR-520 bridge closed eastbound 9am-noon for emergency repair, snarling cross-lake traffic.
